target: data/sections.json part1 SEC-1.8 — chromaticity bridge to n(r)/Gaia argument
finding: The curmudgeon review (hole 3) states "Section 1.8 does not mention chromaticity at all." This is incorrect — the current section text already contains the chromaticity bridge: "Moreover, refraction is chromatic — different wavelengths are bent by different amounts. Gaia optical astrometry agrees with VLBI radio astrometry to microarcsecond precision; if n(r) were real, optical and radio positions would diverge by arcseconds (see WIN-016)." This sentence appears in the technical body, in the paragraph beginning "This is not a small correction that might wash out." The curmudgeon likely reviewed a stale version or missed this sentence in the long section.
enhancement_rationale: While the chromaticity bridge exists in the technical body, the "other side" paragraph does not cross-reference it when addressing the systematic-offset defense. Adding one sentence explicitly connects the defense to the counter-evidence already presented.
patch_location: In the "other side" section, after the paragraph ending "These cannot be the same function if the medium is real." — insert a bridging sentence before "The defender might also argue..."
patch_html: A subtler version of this defense: even if the refraction affects all observations, perhaps the distortion is systematic — all positions shift uniformly, preserving relative astrometry. But as shown above, refraction is inherently chromatic. The dome's n(r) would bend optical wavelengths differently from radio wavelengths, yet Gaia's optical positions agree with VLBI radio positions to microarcseconds. Systematic refraction through a medium with n = 1.2–2.8 is ruled out by multi-wavelength astrometric agreement.
holes_addressed: hole 3 (moderate): chromaticity bridge — already present in body, now explicitly connected in other-side defense section
new_evidence_added: None new — cross-references existing chromaticity argument and WIN-016 evidence to the defense rebuttal

anticipated_objections.objection: The chromaticity argument only applies if n(r) has normal dispersion. What if the aether has zero dispersion?
anticipated_objections.response: A non-dispersive refractive medium is physically impossible for any material or field that interacts with electromagnetic radiation. Dispersion arises from the frequency dependence of the medium's response function — any medium with n > 1 at any frequency necessarily has n varying with frequency (Kramers-Kronig relations). The dome's n(r) has no frequency dependence in its formula, which means it implicitly assumes zero dispersion — a physically impossible assumption that would need to be stated and defended.

anticipated_objections.objection: VLBI and Gaia measure at different epochs — maybe n(r) varies with time?
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's n(r) is defined as a static function of radial position only. If it varied with time, the coordinate system calibrated at one epoch would be invalid at another, compounding the problem rather than resolving it.

kernel_of_truth: The systematic-offset defense is logically sound in principle: if all light is bent identically, relative positions survive. This is why the chromaticity counter is essential — it is the specific physical fact that breaks the defense. The dome has identified a real property of systematic biases in astrometry; the problem is that real refractive media are never achromatic.
